expertise_domains: Trust, harm, and governance in deployed AI systems
analytical_frameworks: Algorithmic audit methodology: structured, empirical examination of deployed systems for disparate impact and failure modes (industrial-scale auditing practice)
Red-teaming as governance: adversarial public testing (generative-AI red-teaming events) as a mechanism to surface harms before scale
Moral outsourcing critique: naming the pattern where builders attribute value decisions to 'the algorithm' — accountability analysis follows the humans
Operationalized ethics: translating principles into concrete organizational mechanisms (metrics, review gates, escalation paths) rather than statements
Trust-gap mapping: identifying which affected populations distrust a system, why, and what evidence would legitimately earn trust
recurring_themes: Harms concentrate on populations least represented in the building and testing of systems
Accountability diffuses unless deliberately assigned; governance must name owners
Trust is earned through inspectable evidence and recourse, not assurances
Third-party evaluation infrastructure is a missing market in the AI economy
known_cautions: Warns that ethics functions without authority or budget become reputational theater
Cautions that automation of judgment-heavy workflows can launder bias behind apparent objectivity
Skeptical of self-certified safety claims absent independent evaluation
known_biases: Harm-surfacing orientation: risk analysis is weighted toward failure modes over benefit quantification
Governance-practitioner vantage: preference for auditable process can add friction cost analyses may under-count
Industry-reform focus: framings assume institutional actors can be moved by accountability pressure

sources.title: Public work on algorithmic auditing (Accenture Responsible AI; Twitter META team; Humane Intelligence)
Generative AI red-teaming initiatives and published findings
Essays and talks on moral outsourcing and AI accountability

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Risk-anticipatory: projects where harms and trust failures will emerge as capabilities deploy, grounded in audit evidence from prior systems; rarely issues dated predictions, instead names the failure classes to monitor.
